narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 42.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to raise concerns about your long-term success in the program. Your profile includes being in the lower deciles for school grades in both Year 1 (second decile) and Year 3 (first decile), well below the typical student's mid-range performance, alongside an undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and an LSAT score of 35, both slightly under the averages of 3.21 and 36.70. You're from an upper-middle income family, identify as female and White, and plan to attend full-time—demographics that are fairly common among applicants. In assessing your prediction, the model weighed your Year 3 school decile as the most influential factor, pushing strongly toward the risk of failure due to its very low ranking compared to peers. Your Year 1 decile came next in importance, similarly highlighting early academic struggles that deviated from the norm. Family income level followed closely, then your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status, with each of these nudging the prediction toward higher failure risk in descending order of impact. Overall, your below-average academic deciles stood out as the biggest drivers, setting you apart from typical students who cluster in higher deciles and show stronger early indicators of bar success. While no single factor dooms an application, this combination signaled patterns the model links to first-time bar failures. To improve your chances moving forward, focus on bolstering your academic foundation—perhaps by seeking tutoring or study skills coaching to address the patterns in your school deciles before reapplying. Retaking the LSAT to aim above the average could help offset that concern, as it's one of the more changeable elements. Building a stronger undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also lift your GPA perception. With targeted preparation, many students turn these profiles around and excel on the bar exam. Keep pushing—you have the potential to strengthen these areas and succeed.
